title: Reuben sandwich
subtitle: Corned beef, sauerkraut, emmental, and Russian dressing on griddled rye
excerpt: The New York deli standard: thinly sliced salt-cured beef brisket, fermented white cabbage, melted emmental, and a mustardy thousand-island-style dressing, pressed between two slices of rye on a buttered griddle. The contrast of sour, salty, and rich is the point.
sourceNotes: The Reuben sandwich's origin is disputed between Reuben Kulakofsky in Omaha (c.1920s) and Reuben's Deli in New York City. The sandwich became a fixture of New York Jewish deli culture in the mid-twentieth century, built on the corned beef and rye bread traditions of Eastern European Jewish immigration. Corned beef brining techniques appear in American domestic literature from the late nineteenth century, including Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The Russian dressing can be made up to 5 days ahead and kept in the fridge.

content.text: A good Reuben requires four things in the right proportions: plenty of thinly sliced corned beef so the filling is generous, sauerkraut that has been pressed dry so the sandwich does not go soggy, emmental or Swiss cheese melted directly over the beef, and a Russian dressing (mayonnaise with ketchup, a little mustard, and a splash of hot sauce) on the bread. The rye bread is not optional; it has the density and flavour to balance the sharp, salty filling.

The dressing takes 2 minutes to make and keeps for a week in the fridge. The sandwich itself is cooked on a buttered skillet, pressed firmly, and served within 2 minutes of coming off the heat. Do not add the sauerkraut before pressing; squeeze it dry first or the bread will not crisp.

items.prepNote: sauerkraut (fermented white cabbage), drained and pressed dry

content.text: Combine the {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise, {{ketchup}} of ketchup, {{wholegrain-mustard}} of wholegrain mustard, and hot sauce in a small bowl. Stir together. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Set aside.

Spread butter on one side of each rye slice. Lay two slices butter-side down in a cold skillet. Spread the dressing on the top face of each. Arrange the corned beef generously over the dressing. Lay the cheese over the beef. Squeeze the sauerkraut firmly in a cloth or between your hands to remove as much liquid as possible, then pile it over the cheese. Top with the remaining bread slices, butter-side up. Set the pan over medium heat. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes per side, pressing firmly with the fish slice, until the rye is dark gold and the cheese has melted.

items.symptom: Bread is soggy on the inside
items.cause: Sauerkraut not pressed dry before adding
items.fix: Squeeze the sauerkraut very firmly to remove liquid before use; any residual moisture will migrate into the bread during cooking

items.cause: Heat too low or cooking time too short
items.fix: Cook on medium heat (not low) and press firmly throughout; if needed, cover the pan for 1 minute after flipping to trap steam and help the cheese melt

items.symptom: Corned beef is tough and dry
items.cause: Pre-cooked corned beef reheated too aggressively on the griddle
items.fix: Use thinly sliced deli-style corned beef; it only needs the residual heat from the griddle — it should not be in contact with direct heat for long

items.cause: Corned beef is already well-seasoned; additional salt in the dressing or sauerkraut compounds it
items.fix: Taste the dressing before assembling and omit any extra salt; rinse the sauerkraut briefly if the brine is particularly sharp

content.text: The Reuben is one of the definitive New York deli sandwiches, built on the corned beef and pickling traditions brought over by Eastern European Jewish immigrants in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. Corned beef (salt-cured beef brisket) was practical and affordable; rye bread was the bread of that community; sauerkraut came from the same fermentation tradition as the pickled cucumbers served on the side. The Russian dressing is the American addition, a concession to the diner palate that softened the sharpness of the sauerkraut with a little sweetness. The sandwich was eventually griddled rather than served cold, and in that form it became a standard on diners and deli menus across the country.
